evidence_quotes.quote: predatory systems (gambling, exploitative marketing) within gaming and online platforms.
end-of-life plans for purchased online services.
transparency of whether or not a product that has been paid for can simply be taken away once more.

paragraphs.text: To whom it may concern, like many others I hereby ask for consideration of digital consumer protection as advocated for by the 'Stop Killing Games' (SKG) initiative to be added to the Digital Fairness Act (DFA). Support for said measures can be found within the European Citizens Initiative titled 'Stop Destroying Videogames' to which I also refer for detailed options on which measures may be taken. In my opinion this suggestion would function well as an extension of some of the digital consumer protection already addressed within the DFA regarding predatory systems (gambling, exploitative marketing) within gaming and online platforms. Simply put, in a digital space filled with predatory microtransactions and subscriptions, consumers are more likely to be continuously investing in online services, many of which they may have already 'bought'. This makes it unacceptable for a digital service to then completely vanish overnight -- as has happens in the past with multiple videogames. In the current climate it is becoming increasingly difficult for a consumer to buy to own in the first place as they are instead forced to 'rent' most products instead. This leaves consumers unprotected and I consider it to be a loophole being exploited. It is also a distinction however is often intentionally made unclear by publishers/hosts of said services. A line needs to be established at which point a service was not a service at all, but instead a short-lived rugpull. The DFA positions itself to enforce transparency between companies and consumers and I believe that there is none greater needed than the transparency of whether or not a product that has been paid for can simply be taken away once more. I hope this puts into context why I believe the Stop Killing Games measures directly correlate to the DFA.The proposed SKG measures cover more than just my singular point above though and demand transparency and end-of-life plans for purchased online services. To be clear, this does not include any utopic demands for all online services to be hosted forever. Some more standard examples would be single player games (often with none or minimal online functions) to not require a server connection and thus lose function as servers are shut down, multiplayer games allowing private servers, or simply making the needed source code and access available to allow consumers the option of organizing their own continued support. Most importantly these end-of-life plans must be publicized by companies in advance so that consumers may make educated purchasing decisions. I thank you for your time and once again strongly urge to reference the Stop Killing Games initiative for much more detailed and thorough propositions than what I could give here and adopt them into the Digital Fairness Act.
